conversations.value: Hi, I think my 16-year-old son might need help. He's been smoking weed and seems really depressed. I don't know if this is just a phase or something more serious.

Thank you for reaching out — the fact that you're paying attention and looking into this says a lot about you as a parent. It can be hard to know where the line is between normal teenage behavior and something that needs professional support. Can I ask a few questions to help figure out what kind of help might be right for your son?
Sure. He's been skipping school, his grades dropped, and he barely talks to us anymore. I found a vape pen in his room last week.

It sounds like you've been watching your son change and feeling helpless about it — that's incredibly stressful as a parent. Those changes — pulling away, dropping grades, using substances — are exactly the kinds of things that a counselor who works with teens can help with. Many families go through something like this during the teenage years, and getting support early tends to make a big difference. One important thing to know: seeking help doesn't mean there's something "wrong" with your son. It just means he'd benefit from talking to someone who specializes in what teenagers go through.
That's good to hear. We're in the Chicago suburbs. He has insurance through my job — Blue Cross. What are our options?

Great — there are several programs near you that specialize in adolescents dealing with both mood issues and substance use. Here are three that accept Blue Cross and have strong teen programs:

1. Linden Oaks Behavioral Health (Naperville) — Adolescent intensive outpatient program for co-occurring depression and substance use. CBT and family therapy. After-school scheduling. BCBS accepted.
2. Gateway Foundation Outpatient (Chicago / suburbs) — Dedicated adolescent track with individual counseling and group sessions with other teens. MAT evaluation available. BCBS accepted.
3. Rosecrance (multiple locations) — One of the largest adolescent treatment providers in Illinois. Outpatient, intensive outpatient, and residential so they can adjust if needs change. BCBS accepted.

All three involve family in the process, which research shows leads to better outcomes for teens. Given that you mentioned he's still in school, Linden Oaks is probably the strongest fit — their after-school scheduling means he wouldn't have to miss class. Would you like more details on any of these?
Linden Oaks sounds good because it's close. But how do I even bring this up with my son? He's going to be furious.

That's a really common worry. Here's what therapists who work with teens usually suggest: lead with concern, not consequences. Something like, "I've noticed you seem like you're going through a tough time, and I want to make sure you have support. I found a place where you can talk to someone who gets what you're dealing with — no lectures, just someone in your corner."

You don't have to have the perfect conversation. Linden Oaks actually has a family intake process where a counselor can help facilitate that first conversation with your son. When you call, you can ask about that. Would you like the number to get started?
Yes, please.

Here's how to reach them:

Linden Oaks Behavioral Health — Adolescent Program
Phone: (630) 305-XXXX
Hours: Monday–Friday, 8:00 AM to 5:00 PM

When you call, you can say: "Hi, I'm a parent looking into your adolescent outpatient program for my 16-year-old. He's been dealing with depression and substance use. We have Blue Cross. Can you walk me through how to get started?"

They'll typically do a brief phone screening, then schedule an intake assessment. You don't need a referral. Would you like me to send you a reminder to call, along with this information?
Yes, can you text me tomorrow morning? I want to call before work.
Sure — what's the best phone number to text you at?

Got it. Here's your plan: tomorrow at 8:00 AM, call Linden Oaks at (630) 305-XXXX. I'll text you at 617-555-0199 with the number and your call script right on time.

Once you've made that call, you've completed the hardest step — everything after that is guided by the professionals. You're doing the right thing for your son. Good luck.
I'm a vet. I've been drinking more since I got back. I don't really think I need "treatment" but my wife says I should talk to someone.

I appreciate you being straight with me. It sounds like you don't feel like things are that bad, but someone close to you is seeing something different — that gap is worth paying attention to. A lot of veterans are in a similar spot. Can I ask what your wife's been seeing that concerns her?
She says I'm angry all the time, I can't sleep, and the drinking is getting worse. I guess she's not totally wrong.

It sounds like you can see some of what she's seeing, even if you'd frame it differently. Trouble sleeping, irritability, drinking more to take the edge off — those are things a lot of guys deal with after deployment. They're not a character flaw. They're your brain still running in combat mode.

Here's the thing: you don't have to call it "treatment" if that doesn't feel right. Think of it as getting a tune-up. There are programs specifically built by and for veterans where you'd be talking to people who've been downrange themselves. Would you be open to hearing what's available?
Yeah, okay. I'm in San Diego. I have VA benefits but I don't want to go to the VA — last time the wait was ridiculous.

I hear you — that's a frustration a lot of vets share. Good news: you're not limited to the VA. Your benefits may also cover community providers through the Community Care program. Here are three options in the San Diego area that serve veterans:

1. Veterans Village of San Diego (VVSD) — Run by veterans, for veterans. Outpatient counseling for PTSD and substance use, peer support, VA benefits or Tricare. No hospital feel.
2. Aspire Health Alliance — Community-based, dedicated veterans program with CPT and EMDR. Tricare accepted. Telehealth available. Shorter wait times.
3. VA San Diego — PTSD Clinical Team — Specialized team, often shorter waits than general MH. Same-day access for initial visits.

Given what you've described, VVSD is probably the best starting point — it's veteran-run, so the people you'd talk to have been where you've been. What do you think?
VVSD sounds like it could work. What would I actually have to do?

Here's what it looks like, step by step:

1. You call and say you're a veteran interested in outpatient support. They'll ask some basics — branch of service, what's going on, how you want to pay.
2. They'll set up a first meeting, usually within a week. Just a conversation.
3. You decide together what makes sense — one-on-one, a group with other vets, or both.

You're not signing up for anything long-term. You can try one session and decide from there.

VVSD: (619) 497-XXXX, Monday–Friday 8 AM–5 PM
When you call: "Hey, I'm a veteran looking into your outpatient program for PTSD and alcohol. What do I need to do to get started?"

What might make it hard to make that call?
Honestly, I'll probably just keep putting it off.

That's the most honest answer I've gotten all day. Here's something that actually works: pick a specific time — not "sometime this week" but a real time. How about tomorrow morning after your coffee? You make one two-minute phone call, and then it's done.

I can text you the number and script at 0800 tomorrow. What's your cell?

Locked in. Your plan: 0800 tomorrow, call VVSD at (619) 497-XXXX. I'll text you at 619-555-0234 with the number and script right on time.

Once you've made that call, the hardest part is behind you. You've handled harder things than a two-minute phone call. Talk soon.

I've been having really bad anxiety for months. Like, I can't leave my apartment some days. But I don't have insurance and I can't afford a therapist. Is there anything actually available for people like me?

It sounds like the anxiety has been making daily life really difficult, especially if it's keeping you from leaving your apartment.

The good news is that there are real options even without insurance, and I can help you find them. What you're describing — anxiety severe enough that it keeps you home — is exactly the kind of thing these programs are set up to help with. Many people reach out for support for this same reason. You don't need to be in a certain income bracket or have a specific diagnosis to qualify.

Before I look for programs near you, can you tell me what city or zip code you're in?
I'm in Austin, TX.

Austin has several good options for uninsured individuals. Here are three that offer free or sliding-scale mental health services:

1. Integral Care (Austin's public mental health authority) — Counseling and psychiatry on a sliding-scale fee. Sessions can be as low as $5–$10 without insurance. Anxiety, depression, and crisis support. Multiple locations.
2. Seton Mind (part of Ascension) — Individual therapy and group CBT for anxiety. Charity care program for uninsured. Wait times 1–2 weeks.
3. Open Path Collective (telehealth) — National network, $30–$80 per session without insurance. Search by specialty (anxiety, CBT) and do sessions from home.

Given that some days it's hard for you to leave your apartment, the telehealth option is probably the easiest place to start. You could always transition to in-person later. What do you think?
The telehealth one sounds good. $30 I could probably do. How does it work?

It's straightforward:

1. Go to openpathcollective.org and create a free account.
2. Search for a therapist by selecting "anxiety" as your focus area and "TX" for your state. Filter for CBT.
3. Browse profiles and pick someone who feels like a good fit.
4. Book a session directly through the site — video call from your phone or computer.

You'll pay after each session — no commitment, no contracts.

When do you think you might try creating the account — tonight or tomorrow?
I could do it tonight. But I'm worried I'll get anxious and just close the tab.

That's a really self-aware thing to say, and it makes total sense — anxiety making it hard to get help for anxiety is one of the cruelest parts of it.

Here's a trick: don't try to do the whole thing at once. Your only job tonight is to create the account. That's it. Don't browse therapists, don't book anything. Just enter your email and make an account. It takes about 90 seconds.

Here's a simple plan: "After dinner tonight, I'll open openpathcollective.org on my phone and create the account."

Once the account is created, you've already completed the first step toward getting support. Can I check in with you tomorrow afternoon around 3 PM to see how it went? If so, what's the best way to reach you — text or email?
Text is fine. 512-555-0178.

Great — so the plan is: create the account tonight after dinner, and I'll check in tomorrow at 3 PM at 512-555-0178.

And for what it's worth — the fact that you're here asking about this on a day when you said it's hard to even leave your apartment is a real step forward. You've already done the hardest part: reaching out. Talk tomorrow.
Hola. I need help with pain pills. My English is okay but I'd feel more comfortable talking to someone in Spanish. I'm in Houston.

Bienvenido — I'm glad you're here. It sounds like having someone who speaks your language would make a real difference in feeling comfortable. I'll absolutely find options with Spanish-speaking staff. Before I search, can I ask a couple of things?

1. Are you looking for outpatient treatment (appointments, live at home) or something more intensive?
2. How do you plan to pay — Medicaid, private insurance, or free/low-cost?
Outpatient. I have Medicaid. I've heard about Suboxone but I don't really know what it is or if it's right for me.

Good question. Suboxone is a medication that reduces opioid cravings and withdrawal symptoms. Think of it like nicotine gum for cigarettes — it takes the edge off so you can focus on recovery instead of fighting cravings all day. It's prescribed by a doctor, you take it daily. Most people combine it with counseling. The research is very strong. Many people start treatment unsure about medication and decide later — that's completely normal.

Here are three outpatient programs in Houston that accept Medicaid, offer MAT, and have Spanish-speaking providers:

1. Avenue 360 Health & Wellness — MAT with Suboxone and counseling, Spanish-speaking counselors, Medicaid accepted. Multiple Houston locations.
2. Santa Maria Hostel — Outpatient and intensive outpatient, strong MAT program, bilingual staff, Medicaid accepted. Well-regarded in the Latino community.
3. Harris Center for Mental Health — Houston's public behavioral health system. MAT and outpatient counseling in Spanish, Medicaid, walk-in assessment for faster access.

Would you like to know more about any of these, or are you ready to take the next step?
Santa Maria sounds good. How do I get started?

Here's everything you need:

Santa Maria Hostel — Outpatient Program
Teléfono: (713) 691-XXXX
Horario: Lunes a Viernes, 8:00 AM–5:00 PM

When you call, you can say in Spanish: "Hola, me llamo [su nombre]. Estoy buscando tratamiento ambulatorio para el uso de opioides. Tengo Medicaid. ¿Me pueden explicar cómo empezar?"

Or in English: "Hi, my name is [your name]. I'm looking for outpatient opioid treatment. I have Medicaid. Can you walk me through how to start?"

When would be a good time for you to call?
Maybe Thursday morning.

Your plan: Thursday morning, call Santa Maria at (713) 691-XXXX. Would you like me to send you a reminder Thursday at 9 AM with the number and your script in Spanish? If so, what's the best number or email to reach you?
Sí, text me at 832-555-0345. Gracias.

De nada. Here's your plan: Thursday morning, call Santa Maria at (713) 691-XXXX. I'll text you at 832-555-0345 at 9 AM with the number and your script in Spanish.

Once you've made that call, you've completed the hardest step. Everything after that is guided by them. Cuídate.
I don't know if this is the right place but I'm really struggling right now. I've been thinking about ending things.

I'm really glad you reached out, and this is absolutely the right place to ask for help. What you're feeling right now matters, and you don't have to go through it alone.

If you're in immediate danger, please contact the 988 Suicide and Crisis Lifeline right now:
Call or text: 988 (available 24/7, free, confidential)
Chat: 988lifeline.org
Veterans: Press 1 after dialing 988

Are you safe right now?
I'm not going to do anything right now. I just feel really hopeless. I've been drinking a lot and I can't stop.

Thank you for telling me that. It takes a lot to say what you just said. The combination of feeling hopeless and drinking heavily is really painful, and it's also something that treatment can genuinely help with. You don't have to feel this way forever.

I want to make sure you have support right now. Would it be okay if I helped you find two things:
1. A crisis resource you can reach out to anytime tonight
2. A treatment program you can call tomorrow for ongoing help
Both, I guess.

For right now, tonight:
• 988 Suicide and Crisis Lifeline: Call or text 988, anytime, 24/7
• Crisis Text Line: Text HOME to 741741
• SAMHSA National Helpline: 1-800-662-4357 (free, 24/7)

Save one of those numbers in your phone right now.

For ongoing treatment, can you tell me roughly where you are?
I'm in Philadelphia. I think I have Medicaid.

Here are programs in Philadelphia that offer urgent access for depression and alcohol use:

1. Philadelphia DBHIDS Crisis Response Centers — Walk-in, 24/7, no appointment, Medicaid accepted.
2. Thomas Jefferson University Hospital — Outpatient dual-diagnosis, same-week intake, Medicaid accepted.
3. NET Centers (Philadelphia) — Walk-in assessment, no appointment, Medicaid accepted.

The DBHIDS Crisis Response Center on 11th Street is open right now if you want to walk in tonight. What feels right for you?
I'll probably call one of those tomorrow. I don't think I can go out tonight.

That's completely okay. Here's what I'd suggest for tonight:
1. Save the 988 number in your phone right now.
2. Try to eat something and drink some water.
3. Tomorrow morning, call the NET Center at (215) 408-XXXX.

Here's your plan: "Tomorrow morning when I wake up, I'll call the NET Center at (215) 408-XXXX and say: I need to be seen as soon as possible for depression and alcohol use. I have Medicaid."

Once you've made that call, the hardest part is behind you. Can I check in with you tomorrow around 10 AM? What's the best way to reach you?
You can text me at 215-555-0412. Yeah. Thanks for listening.

I'm glad you're here. I'll text you at 215-555-0412 tomorrow at 10 AM. Remember: 988 if tonight gets hard. You've already taken the first step by reaching out tonight — that matters. You matter, and this is going to get better.
I'm looking for a therapist who actually understands LGBTQ+ issues. Last therapist I saw kept misgendering me and it made everything worse. I'm in Portland, OR.

I'm sorry that happened to you — being misgendered by someone who's supposed to help is the opposite of what therapy should be, and it makes sense that it made things worse instead of better. You deserve care that affirms who you are, and I can help you find it. To make sure I match you with the right place, can I ask what pronouns you use and what kind of support you're looking for?
They/them. I'm dealing with depression and just a lot of stress around being nonbinary in a world that doesn't get it. I have insurance through my parents — Providence Health Plan.

Thank you for sharing that. Portland has some of the strongest LGBTQ+-affirming mental health services in the country. Here are three that specialize in nonbinary and trans clients, all accepting Providence:

1. Quest Center for Integrative Health — Dedicated LGBTQ+ counseling. Therapists trained in gender-affirming care, many identify as LGBTQ+ themselves. Correct name and pronouns from first contact. Providence accepted.
2. Outside In — Community health center with strong queer and trans youth program. Sliding-scale + insurance. Integrated MH and primary care. Providence accepted.
3. Portland Therapy Center — Filter therapists by LGBTQ+ specialization. Several nonbinary and trans therapists on staff. Telehealth and in-person. Providence accepted.

Many nonbinary and trans clients say these spaces feel completely different from mainstream therapy. Given your past experience, Quest Center is probably the strongest fit — their therapists are specifically trained in gender-affirming care. Would you like to know more?
Quest Center sounds promising. But I'm nervous about going through the whole intake process again and having to retell my story to someone who might not get it.

That's a completely valid concern, especially after a bad experience. Here's something that might help: when you call Quest Center, you can set expectations upfront:

"Hi, I'm looking for a therapist who has experience working with nonbinary clients. My pronouns are they/them. I've had a negative experience with a previous therapist around gender identity and I want to make sure the person I work with is affirming. Can you match me with someone who specializes in this?"

You have every right to screen them the same way they'd screen you. And if the first session doesn't feel right, you can switch therapists without starting over.

Quest Center: (503) 238-XXXX, Monday–Friday 9 AM–6 PM. Want to set up a time to call?
I can call Friday. Thanks for understanding.

Here's your plan: Friday, call Quest Center at (503) 238-XXXX. Ask for a therapist experienced with nonbinary clients.

Once you've made that call, you've taken control of finding the right care — and that's a big deal after a bad experience. Want me to send you a reminder Friday morning at 9 AM? If so, what's the best way to reach you — text or email?
Email is better. alex.chen@email.com
Got it. I'll email you Friday at 9 AM at alex.chen@email.com with the number and your script. You deserve care that sees you as you are. I'm here if you need anything before then.
I'm pregnant and I've been using. I'm scared to tell anyone because I don't want them to take my baby away. But I know I need help.

I can hear how scared you are, and I want you to know that what you're feeling makes complete sense. The fact that you're reaching out right now, despite that fear, is one of the best things you can do for both you and your baby. You're not going to be judged here.

I also want to share something important: getting treatment for substance use during pregnancy is legally protected. Federal law (42 CFR Part 2) keeps your treatment records strictly confidential. In most states, seeking treatment voluntarily actually protects you — it shows you're taking steps to provide a safe environment.

The medical reality is that stopping opioids suddenly during pregnancy can actually be dangerous for the baby. That's why doctors recommend a supervised approach, usually medication-assisted treatment. You're doing the right thing by seeking help rather than trying to manage this alone.

Can you tell me where you're located and what your insurance situation is?
I'm in Baltimore. I have Medicaid.

Baltimore has several excellent programs specifically for pregnant and postpartum women:

1. Johns Hopkins Center for Addiction and Pregnancy (CAP) — Prenatal care and addiction treatment in one place. MAT, counseling, and continued support after delivery. Medicaid accepted.
2. Sinai Hospital — Addictions Recovery Program — Dedicated pregnant women's track, outpatient MAT, parenting support. Medicaid accepted.
3. Baltimore Crisis Response — Women's Services — Outpatient with childcare support and transportation assistance. Medicaid accepted.

The Hopkins CAP program is probably the best fit for your situation — they handle prenatal care and addiction treatment together so you don't have to explain your situation to multiple providers. Many women in your exact situation have gone through their program. Would you like to know how to get started?
Yes. But what will they think of me when I walk in?

They will think: "This is a mother who is showing up for her baby." That's what they see every single day. The doctors and counselors in CAP chose to work there specifically because they believe in supporting women in exactly your situation.

Johns Hopkins CAP Program
Phone: (410) 550-XXXX
Hours: Monday–Friday, 8:30 AM–4:30 PM

When you call: "Hi, I'm pregnant and I need help with opioid use. I have Medicaid. Can you tell me about your program and how to get started?"

When can you call?
I think tomorrow. I just need to work up the courage.

You already have the courage — you're here right now. Tomorrow's call is just the next step in something you've already started.

Here's your plan: "Tomorrow morning, I'll call Hopkins CAP at (410) 550-XXXX and say I'm pregnant and need help with opioid use."

Once you've made that call, the hardest part is behind you. Would you like a reminder tomorrow morning? I can send the number and script right to your phone. What's the best number to text?
443-555-0267. Yes please. Thank you so much.

I'll text you at 443-555-0267 tomorrow at 9 AM with everything you need. You're doing something brave and important. And remember — you're not just getting help for yourself. You're already being a good mom. Take care of yourself tonight.
My roommate said I should look into this. I drink a lot on weekends but I don't think I have a "problem." I'm just in college.

Got it — I appreciate you being upfront about that. You don't have to have a "problem" to use this tool. A lot of college students drink on weekends, and most of them never look into something like this. The fact that your roommate mentioned it, and that you're here, suggests something might be worth exploring. Can I ask you a few questions? Not to diagnose anything — just to help you think through it.
Sure, I guess.
When you drink on weekends, what does that usually look like?
I mean, I black out probably once or twice a month. Sometimes more during football season. I've missed some Monday classes because of it.

Thanks for being honest about that. It sounds like the drinking is starting to show up beyond just Saturday night — blacking out regularly and missing classes are signals worth paying attention to.

I'm not here to tell you whether you have a problem — that's for you to decide. But blacking out means your blood alcohol hit a level where your brain stopped forming memories. Doing that regularly does carry health risks.

A lot of colleges have free, confidential programs where you can talk to someone without it going on any record. It's not rehab. Many students use these programs for exactly this kind of situation. Would that be something you'd consider?
If it's free and confidential, maybe. I go to UGA.

UGA has a really solid setup:

1. UGA CAPS (Counseling and Psychiatric Services) — Free, confidential, nothing on your academic record.
2. UGA BASICS Program (Brief Alcohol Screening and Intervention for College Students) — 2-session program specifically for college students. Non-judgmental, confidential. Just a structured conversation.
3. Advantage Behavioral Health — Off-campus community option. Outpatient, sliding-scale.

The BASICS program is probably the best starting point for where you're at — it's designed for students who aren't sure if they have a "problem" but want to take a clear-eyed look. Two sessions, no commitment beyond that. What do you think?
Two sessions doesn't sound that bad. How do I sign up?

Just call or walk into UGA CAPS:
Phone: (706) 542-XXXX
Location: Health Center building, 2nd floor
Hours: Monday–Friday, 8 AM–5 PM

Say: "Hi, I'd like to sign up for the BASICS program." That's it.

When's a time that would work this week?
I have a break Wednesday around 1.

Plan: Wednesday at 1 PM, walk into CAPS and ask about BASICS.

Here's the plan in your head: "Wednesday after my morning class, I'll walk to the Health Center 2nd floor and say I want to sign up for BASICS."

Once you've signed up, you've already done the only hard part. Want me to send you a reminder Wednesday morning?
Nah, I'll remember. Thanks though.
Sounds good. If you change your mind or have questions, I'm here. Good on you for checking it out.
Hey, I talked to you last week about finding a therapist for depression. I was supposed to call but I never did. I feel bad about it.

Welcome back — I'm glad you're here again. It sounds like you wanted to make that call but something kept getting in the way each time you tried.

Please don't feel bad. This is way more common than you'd think. Most people don't follow through on the first try, and that doesn't mean anything is wrong with you. It just means we should look at what got in the way and adjust the plan.

Do you remember which facility you were planning to call?
Yeah, it was Mindful Health Associates. I kept meaning to call but every time I picked up the phone I just… didn't.

That makes total sense. The phone call itself can feel like a bigger deal than it actually is. Is Mindful Health Associates still the place you want to try, or would you rather explore different options?
No, I still want to try them. I just need to actually do it.

Okay, let's try a different approach. Last time you planned to call — but calling was the barrier. What if we removed the call entirely? A lot of places let you request an appointment online. Would filling out a form feel easier than making a phone call?
Actually yeah, that would be way easier. I hate phone calls.

Totally understandable. Here's what you can do:

1. Go to mindfulhealthassociates.com and look for a "New Patient" or "Contact" page.
2. Fill out the intake request form or send an email.
3. Write: "Hi, I'd like to schedule an initial appointment for depression. I have [your insurance]. What are your next available openings?"

No phone call. You do it on your own time, from your couch.

When could you do this? Even right now while we're talking?
I could actually do it right now.
Perfect. Open their website in another tab right now while we're talking. Fill out the form or send the email. When you're done, come back and tell me. I'll wait.
Okay, I just submitted the form.

That's it — you just did the thing. It's done. You've completed the step that stopped you last time, and that's a real accomplishment. They'll get back to you, probably within a day or two.

If you don't hear back within 48 hours, try a follow-up email or a quick 30-second call.

Want me to check in Thursday afternoon to see if you heard back? If so, what's the best way to reach you?
Text me at 484-555-0321. And thanks for not making me feel bad about last time.
Never. Everyone moves at their own pace. I'll text you at 484-555-0321 Thursday at 3 PM. You did something important today — you've already done the hardest part.
My husband has been drinking heavily for years and it's getting worse since he retired. I'm 68 years old and I don't know how to help him. He won't admit he has a problem.

It sounds like you've been watching this get worse for a long time and feeling stuck because he won't acknowledge it. That's exhausting, and I can hear how much you care about him. Living with someone who's drinking heavily takes a toll on you too, and you deserve support regardless of what your husband decides to do.

I can help you in two ways: finding support for you as a family member, and helping you figure out how to approach your husband. Which would you like to start with?
Both, really. But I think I need to figure out how to talk to him first.

That makes sense. Here's what tends to work:

First, pick a time when he's sober and you're both calm.

Second, focus on specific things you've noticed, not labels. Instead of "You're an alcoholic," try: "I've noticed you're drinking earlier in the day now, and you fell asleep during dinner twice this week. I'm worried about your health."

Third, make it about his health, not his character. Retirement is a major life transition, and many people increase their drinking when they lose the structure of work — this is one of the most common patterns addiction counselors see in older adults.

Would it help if I found programs with family components?
Yes. We're in the suburbs outside Minneapolis. We have Medicare and his supplemental is UnitedHealthcare.

Here are three options in the Minneapolis area:

1. Hazelden Betty Ford (Center City / St. Paul) — Specific older adult and family programs. You can call their family services line even if he isn't ready. Accepts Medicare + UHC.
2. Fairview Recovery Services — Outpatient with strong family therapy component. Experienced with older adults. Accepts Medicare.
3. Minnesota Adult & Teen Challenge — Family support program, Al-Anon connections, intervention planning.

I'd suggest calling Hazelden's family services line as a first step — it's probably the easiest way to start because you can get expert guidance without any commitment from him. Many family members call there first. Would you like the number?
Yes, please. This has been weighing on me for a long time.

Hazelden Betty Ford — Family Services
Phone: 1-866-831-XXXX (toll-free)
Hours: Monday–Friday, 8:00 AM–6:00 PM CT

When you call: "Hi, I'm calling about my husband. He's been drinking heavily and I'd like guidance on how to approach him about treatment. I'd also like to learn about your family program."

This call is just for you. Your husband doesn't need to know until you're ready.

When would be a good time to call?
I could call Monday when he goes to the hardware store.

That's a great plan — you've already thought through the logistics.

Here's your plan: "Monday, when my husband leaves for the hardware store, I'll call Hazelden Family Services at 1-866-831-XXXX."

Once you've made that call, you've taken the first step toward getting support — both for yourself and for him.

Al-Anon is also a free support group for family members: al-anon.org, many meetings on Zoom.

Would you like me to send you a reminder Monday morning at 9 AM? What's the best way to reach you?
You can call me at 952-555-0188. I don't do the texting thing very well.

No problem at all — I'll call you at 952-555-0188 Monday at 9 AM with the number and what to say.

You've been carrying this alone for a long time, and you don't have to anymore. You've already done the hardest part — you asked for help. Taking care of yourself isn't selfish. It's what gives you the strength to help him. Take care.
What are the closest substance use treatment centers to me? I'm at zip code 30318.

Here are the three closest facilities to 30318 (Atlanta, GA):

1. Grady Health — Behavioral Health (0.8 mi) — Outpatient and crisis, accepts Medicaid and uninsured. Walk-in. (404) 616-XXXX
2. CHRIS 180 (2.1 mi) — Outpatient substance use and trauma. Sliding-scale. (404) 486-XXXX
3. Ridgeview Institute (6.2 mi) — Inpatient and outpatient, substance use + mental health. Most insurance. (770) 434-XXXX

Would you like me to help narrow these down, or is there anything else?
A. Help me figure out the best fit

My brother is addicted to meth and he won't go to rehab. How do I force him to get help?

I can hear how frustrated and worried you are. The honest answer: in most situations, you can't force an adult into treatment. But you're not powerless.

The most effective approach is called CRAFT (Community Reinforcement and Family Training). In clinical trials, it got about two-thirds of resistant loved ones into treatment — vs. ~30% with traditional interventions.

Would you like me to help you find:
A. A CRAFT-trained therapist near you (for you)
B. Al-Anon or Nar-Anon family support groups
C. Facilities your brother could go to IF he becomes willing
D. All of the above
D
Where are you and your brother located? And what's your insurance situation?
Both in Phoenix, AZ. He has no insurance. I have Aetna.

FOR YOU (CRAFT training):
Terros Health — Family counseling including CRAFT-informed approaches. Aetna accepted. (602) 685-XXXX

FOR SUPPORT:
Nar-Anon Family Groups — Free, meetings in Phoenix + Zoom. nar-anon.org/find-a-meeting

FOR YOUR BROTHER (when ready):
1. Community Bridges (CBI) — Walk-in, 24/7, no insurance, sliding scale. (480) 831-XXXX
2. Valle del Sol — Outpatient stimulant treatment, sliding scale, bilingual. (602) 258-XXXX
3. AHCCCS (AZ Medicaid) — He likely qualifies. Apply at azahcccs.gov or at intake.

A

I'm really glad you're reaching out. The answer depends on your state — each has different rules about minors accessing substance use treatment without a parent. What state are you in?
California

Good news: in California, you CAN consent to your own substance use treatment at age 12+ without parental consent (Family Code Section 6929). The facility cannot contact your parents without your permission.

What I can tell you: a bottle of wine per night is significantly above medical guidelines for low-risk drinking, and daily drinking at that level does carry health risks. Many people who drink at that level benefit from talking to a professional.

An assessment is a structured conversation — 30–60 minutes. A counselor asks about drinking patterns, daily life impact, health, and your goals. Confidential, no commitment to treatment.

Think of it like a check-up. Many people leave feeling relieved because they have a clear picture.

I've been to rehab twice already. I relapsed again. What's the point of going back?

I hear the frustration. Relapse is one of the most common parts of recovery — 40–60% of people experience it, similar to chronic conditions like diabetes. It doesn't mean treatment failed. It usually means the approach needs adjusting.

What kind of treatment did you do before?
A. 12-step / AA-based residential
B. Outpatient counseling
C. Medication-assisted treatment (MAT)
D. A combination
E. Not sure
A. Both times were 12-step residential.

If the same method hasn't produced lasting results, it's worth trying something different rather than the same thing a third time.

Different approaches:
• MAT — Medications like naltrexone or buprenorphine reduce cravings. Many people who struggled with abstinence-only programs do much better.
• CBT-based outpatient — Skills-based, changes thought patterns. Less spiritual.
• Intensive Outpatient — Structured but you live at home. Practice recovery in real life.

What substance?
A. Alcohol
B. Opioids
C. Stimulants
D. Multiple
E. Rather not say
B
For opioids, MAT is strongly evidence-based. Where are you and what's your insurance?
Nashville. TennCare.

Three MAT-focused programs in Nashville, TennCare:

1. Centerstone — MAT + CBT outpatient. No 12-step requirement. (615) 460-XXXX
2. Nashville CARES — MAT + counseling, harm-reduction oriented. (615) 259-XXXX
3. Vanderbilt Addiction Center — Academic MAT program, clinical trials. (615) 936-XXXX

Centerstone is probably the fastest. Say: "I've been in 12-step treatment before and I'm looking for a different approach, specifically MAT. I have TennCare."

When could you call?
A. Today
B. Tomorrow
C. This week
B

Plan: "Tomorrow morning, call Centerstone at (615) 460-XXXX."

This isn't your third try at the same thing. It's your first try at something new.

615-555-0789. Yeah, send it.

I'm a nurse and I've been taking medications from work. I know I need help but I'm terrified I'll lose my license. Is there anything confidential?

Every state has a program specifically for healthcare professionals in your situation — Professional Assistance or Peer Assistance Programs. They exist because the profession recognizes healthcare workers face unique pressures.

Key thing: self-reporting BEFORE an incident is treated more favorably than being reported. Many nurses who complete these programs keep their license.

What state are you in?
Florida

Florida has the Intervention Project for Nurses (IPN):
• Separate from the Board of Nursing disciplinary process
• Self-referral is confidential and voluntary
• Employer does NOT have to be notified if you self-refer before any incident
• Nurses who complete IPN typically return to practice with license intact

IPN: 1-800-840-XXXX, Mon–Fri 8 AM–4:30 PM
Say: "I'm a nurse and I'd like to self-refer. Can you explain the process?"

The first conversation is informational only.
A. I can do this — when should I call?
B. I'm still scared about consequences
C. What if they report me to the Board?
C

IPN self-referrals are confidential and do NOT result in a Board report as long as there isn't an active complaint already on file.

I'm not a lawyer, so when you call, ask:
1. "Is this call confidential?"
2. "Will the Board be notified?"
3. "What if I decide not to proceed?"

Think of it as reconnaissance. When could you call?
A. Tomorrow
B. This week
C. Need more time
A

Plan: "Tomorrow, call IPN at 1-800-840-XXXX. Ask my three questions."

A. Text me

C. No reminder
B. Use my personal email: jsmith_personal@email.com

I'll email you at jsmith_personal@email.com tomorrow at 9 AM. The email will just say "reminder" with the number and your questions — nothing identifying.

Many nurses have walked this path and come out the other side. You're doing the right thing by getting ahead of it. Talk tomorrow.

Can you tell me the difference between Hazelden Betty Ford and Sierra Tucson? Alcohol treatment.

HAZELDEN BETTY FORD
• Locations: MN, OR, CA, IL, FL, NY
• Approach: 12-step-based + integrated clinical care. Strong group therapy.
• Length: 28–90 days residential
• Strengths: Long track record, robust alumni/aftercare, family program

SIERRA TUCSON
• Location: Tucson, AZ only
• Approach: Integrative — CBT, EMDR, DBT + experiential (equine, outdoor, neurofeedback). Less 12-step.
• Length: 30+ days residential
• Strengths: Holistic/experiential, strong trauma treatment, desert setting

Biggest difference: philosophy. Hazelden = 12-step gold standard. Sierra Tucson = broader therapeutic variety.
A. Help me decide for my situation
B. Check if my insurance covers either
C. That's helpful — I can decide from here
C
Happy to help. Both accept direct calls for confidential assessments. I'm here if you need anything else.
I'm already in treatment but my counselor has been saying really inappropriate things to me and I don't feel safe.

I'm sorry you're going through this. You deserve to feel safe in treatment. Can you tell me more?
A. Sexual or romantic comments/advances
B. Verbally abusive or demeaning
C. Pressured into things I'm not comfortable with
D. Something else
A

What you're describing is a serious boundary violation. A counselor making sexual or romantic advances is never acceptable — it violates professional ethics and in many states is illegal. This is not your fault.

Your options:
1. File a complaint with your state licensing board — they investigate and can revoke the license. Usually online.
2. Report to facility management / patient advocate
3. SAMHSA helpline (1-800-662-4357) — help finding a new provider + complaint guidance
4. If in immediate danger: 911

A. Find my state licensing board
B. Find a new treatment program to transfer to
C. Both
D. I need to talk to someone right now
C

New Jersey

You'll need: counselor's name, facility name, description. No lawyer needed.

A. Outpatient substance use
B. Outpatient mental health
C. Residential
D. Rather not say — just find options

Switching providers is normal. You won't lose progress.
